text: Recent studies suggest that the intestinal microbiota influences gut brain axis and this study further investigates the impact of the gut microbiota on the brain. Wild type specific pathogen free (SPF) mice were either used as is or were first made as germ free (GF) mice, then colonized with microbiota from SPF mice (Ex-GF mice) and subsequent comparisons of their brain metabolomic profiles between GF and Ex-GF mice were carried out by using CE-TOFMS. The brain profiles were found to contain 196 features. Compared to GF mice, the amount of 38 features (metabolites) were increased or decreased significantly in Ex-GF mice, about 10 of which are known to be involved with brain function and a large group of unknown composition. The study showed unexpectedly that the intestinal microbiota had a link with glucose metabolism of the brain, and the domestic microbiota significantly affected the brain metabolome through the newly discovered microbiota-gut-brain axis, and the colonization of the brain was critical for the growth of the brain, the performance of brain functions, behavior and the maintenance of normal brain behaviors.
